story:
School let out and my crazy brother Ted and I spilled into the sun with armloads of finished papers and workbooks and a plan—as always. We fed the burn barrel, ash puffing up like tiny phoenixes, and whooped as fire ate a year’s worth of homework. Our minds leapt ahead to June, July, August—none of it to be wasted.

June meant the creek below our house ran fast with spring melt. We swapped school clothes for swimsuits and headed to the wood yard, where Ted charmed two discarded pallets from a worker. Earlier we’d collected eight empty milk bags from the school kitchen, the kind that sat in the cooler with rubber spouts—trash bound, now our air bladders. We rinsed them, blew them full, and tied them off. Four bags fit snug between the pallet slats. One pallet plus four air bags equaled a raft. We built two by afternoon’s end and scavenged saplings for poles, naming the next day our maiden voyage.

The next morning, Mom packed us sandwiches and gave the one hard rule: be back by dinner. In a family of eight, with friends from big families a bike ride away, there was always something to do, and it was usually outside. The main action gathered at our place, largely because of Ted—funny, wild, loyal—ten that summer to my nine.

Dad had fenced the creek so little ones wouldn’t tumble in, but that day the water ran nearly level with the top wire, brown and urgent, pushing up into the pasture and racing toward the highway culvert. We dragged and carried the heavy pallets along the dusty track, sweat sticky, stubborn as mules. After a mile we cut down through long grass to the swollen bank. We were part Kon-Tiki in our heads—Thor Heyerdahl on balsa, the Pacific wide open.

Six or seven friends trailed us, eager until they heard the roar of the current. Ted invited them aboard with his usual grin. Nobody took him up. I wasn’t sure the rafts would float. They were so heavy, and the water loud.

We waded in anyway. A friend handed me my pole. On my knees, my pallet floated—barely. Ted’s raft bumped mine. “Stand up on it!” he shouted over the rush. He was Thor in a storm, and I stood.

The current seized us. We were up, then down, pallets drowning and rising, water sheeting over the slats, licking our ankles, then knees. We’d thrown in with the river. Ted’s face, when he looked back, glowed. Living, it said. We flew past our house, flashed by the pasture. Another second and—

The sound grew into a roar. Ahead, the highway culvert, usually a summer playground when the creek ran low and we could wade through to the rich neighbor’s lake with swans and tidy paths, was a gaping throat. Floodwater funneled down, deafening. We were aimed at it.

“Swim to safety!” Ted yelled, stabbing his pole toward a tuft of bank. The current laughed and held him. He jumped. I jumped after, panic cold and total. The rafts, poles, and milk bags vanished into the culvert’s mouth. We didn’t see the splinters shoot out the far side; we were fighting for grass, fingers in the roots, water tugging at our feet.

We hauled ourselves onto the soggy bank, coughing, shaking, alive. Ted sat up, grinned. “That was something,” he said. “We made it.”

We jogged back toward our friends, already turning the terror into a tale. Ted could spin a story like no one else. Another summer day, another near-miss, a doozy to tell—Kon-Tiki on a creek, and we were still here to laugh about it.
